FWIW, I typically trace the Three Rays of Light over my food and say something like "May the blessings of the divine be upon this food and upon all those who have given of themselves that I might eat."

When cooking, I do something similar, except I sometimes pray to specific deities when it's appropriate. I bake bread every day, and I always treat this as a sacramental rite. I pray the following over dough after it's been set to rise and over the loaf after it comes out of the oven:

Hu the Mighty, Great Druid God, enlighten me through Thy initiation.
I give thanks for this bread, the gift of Ced, Mother of the Earth and lady of Grains; of Hesus, Chief of tree Spirits, Lord of Green Life and my patron deity; of Sul, lady of the Threefold Fire, who guards the hearthfire of this kitchen.
In the presence of the Holy Ones, let this bread be blessed in the Three Rays of Light (trace /|\), and let it nourish and sustain all who will eat of it.
Vibrate: AWEN.
Hu the mighty, Great Druid God, enlighten me through Thy initiation.

When I cut the first piece of a new loaf, I offer a portion to the gods, and consume the first bite slowly and reverently.

Something similar could be adapted into a general grace before meals, if you were so inclined.

The Dolmen Arch is old-fashioned American occultism with a Druid flavor -- it includes some magical techniques but it's not primarily about ceremonial magic. It contains a lot of philosophy and symbolism, and an energy healing system a little like reiki.

The Celtic Golden Dawn is full-on ceremonial magic in the Golden Dawn tradition, but invoking Celtic deities and working with Druid philosophy instead of the Judeo-Christian god names and symbolism of the Hermetic GD.

comments.title: Bears and Banishing
comments.content:
Salutations JMG! If the snow has made it out to Rhode Island I hope you're enjoying some cozy inside time. It's already shin-deep here in BC.

1) Does the LBRP banish the sulfur of spring rain water? I know you've said that herbs and natural materials keep their essential nature despite banishing, but what about materials that hold a charge that can be dissapated if it hits the ground, such as spring water? For that matter, is there anything in the Philosophers of Nature Spagyrics course that interacts poorly with the LBRP?

2) Is there any Hoodoo, Prayers, or folk magic for protection from wild animals? I've already run into a cougar while coming down a mountain in the pitch black (while lost and dehydrated, long story) and I don't want to repeat the experience. I'm knowledgable in predator psychology, I carry a huge can of high-strength capsicum spray, and I constantly keep my head on a swivel when the forest goes quiet. But after a certain point you have to bed down for the night (preferably away from a game trail) and just hope that if a predator wanders by in the night, it's not habituated to humans and leaves your sleeping bivvy alone. My worst fear is cresting a hill to find a territorial male brown bear getting ready to charge. That happened to my mother once, and she only escaped mauling by climbing into a JCB excavator because she was already at a remote construction site. I know dangerous predator encounters are fairly rare, but is there anything to make such encounters even rarer?

replies.title: Re: Bears and Banishing
replies.content:
1) It can; I don't recommend doing magical practices in the same room where you do your alchemical workings. If you keep them separate, though, you should be fine.

2) I don't happen to know of anything like that. Anyone else?

2) Would Coel be able to help? The CGD describes him as a "huntsman" and presumably hunters have to deal with predators, but maybe that would result in the opposite outcome (lots of bear and wolf pelts coming my way, ruhroh!) I assume Pan would also be a bad choice? Especially considering his connection with satyrs makes him...not such a protective type.

2) Coel is the Master of Animals, thus potentially a good choice -- but it depends on what he chooses. Pan -- no, definitely not, as he also has a reputation for trying to scare people half to death.

replies.title: Re: Bears and Banishing
replies.content: The usual procedures for bears and cougars – clean campsite, hang smellables in bear bags, different clothes for cooking and the rest of the day, pepper spray, a camp fire, garden dog-cat-rabbit repellant, ammonia and citrus scents.  I don’t know of any magic that might help beyond general good luck stuff.  Predators tend to avoid humans in the US due to the popularity of Kipling’s boom-stick… except the cougar that made friends with my neighbor’s cat and moved in…

replies.title: Re: Bears and Banishing
replies.content:
2) I have heard a couple things that might be helpful. I stayed with a homesteader who seemed to have an agreement with the local bears—possibly he told them that he'd shoot and eat them if they messed with him. He kept food in flimsy totes in an outdoor kitchen, and there was an outdoor compost pile for meat scraps full of maggots. Bears never touched either for 25 years, even though they'd come eat the dandelions in the grass several meters away (I saw this). Talking to the animals seems like a possible approach. Also, I think a native lady in Ontario once told me something about sprinkling cedar chips in a circle around one's camp to keep out bears—I am not sure if there was more to it than that or not.

~Bewilderness

comments.title: Planetary Charity on Behalf of Others?
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I was thinking about Planetary Charity. It seems to my logical side, which only has a sometimes-correct relationship to spiritual truths or general effectiveness, that it might work something like this: somebody born with planets in hostile positions has the more difficult part of the spectrum of those planets' energies coming at them their entire life. This can play out in any number of ways, but it will play out in one way or the other. Planetary Charity may not be so much about pleasing Planetary Deities (although it may be that as well), so much as providing the most benign way possible for the person doing the charitable act to make a connection with that planet's energies. Any blowback will be in the form of gifts, and in this case the gift will evidently take the form of lessing a maleficent effect. Thus acts that are not necessary in the form of monetary donations, if they are done as forms of blessing or charity towards others working through that planet's energies, may be just as effective, though it may take some experimenting to see what would work. Does this all seem like a reasonable line of thought so far?

My next question is, do you think that in theory it would it be possible to perform acts of planetary charity (whether monetary or otherwise) but to dedicate any positive outcomes not to oneself but to somebody else?

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity on Behalf of Others?
replies.content:
1) That seems plausible, though it would require experimentation, as you suggest, to see if it actually works.

2) I have no idea. I tend to think it would depend on the character and mood of the planetary intelligence.

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity on Behalf of Others?
replies.content:
1) That seems plausible, though it would require experimentation, as you suggest, to see if it actually works.

Recently I've seen a couple of blogs by different authors suggesting a few such methods of non-monetary planetary acts of charity, although how much they have tested the methods out for themselves, I am not sure. It occurs to me that the emotional and physical distance inherent in sending physical or monetary donations rather than doing more direct acts of service might actually be a feature rather than a bug when it comes to afflicted planets; this keeps more material layers between the affected person and the afflicted planet for the energies in relation to that person to ground out before they reach their target on the material plane. In which case, in cases of planetary affliction, experimenting with other forms of charity might, in fact, be a way to get burned by that planet's energies directly (e.g. helping drug addicts and in the process being exposed to drugs one is attracted and weak to in the process, or some such) and should be approached with caution.

Does this line of thought also sound reasonable, according to the theory as you understand it?

2) I have no idea. I tend to think it would depend on the character and mood of the planetary intelligence.


In the question of trying to dedicate planetary charity towards someone who has an afflicted malefic planet, would you say there is a risk that even attempting such a thing something could potentially backfire and bring trouble on oneself that wasn't there to begin with? And is divination the best approach to answering the question of whether such an attempt is likely to succeed?

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity on Behalf of Others?
replies.content:
1) Yes, that seems plausible.

2) Yes and yes.

comments.title: Non-Abrahamic magical systems
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm interested in starting magical practice, but a sticking point for me has been that a lot of the systematized magical thinking codified in recent Western history is based on Abrahamic concepts. Many of the rituals, meditative practices and symbol systems involve Abrahamic deities and spiritual frameworks.

I feel no resonance with the Abrahamic egregores. What I see is that at their core, they are based on holy books which assert an absolutist cosmology that flies in the face of my own experience. Magical practitioners often seem to invoke Abrahamic powers in concert with other powers and spiritual systems and this directly contradicts the assertions of the holy books that they contain the perfect, exclusive truth, not to mention their explicit bans on the use of magic. Perhaps these practitioners have another perspective, but from my perspective I cannot coherently draw on powers with which I have no affinity while acting in direct contradiction to the doctrines that are the basis of how almost all their believers experience them.

The methodical, disciplined nature of systems like Golden Dawn is appealing to me, much more so than a fluffy Wiccan spellbook. Your SoP text prescribes that you vibrate the names of whatever deities or powers you find appropriate; is this approach broadly applicable to the practices of modern ritual magic? Will I face any roadblocks pursuing magical practice while playing Mad Libs in such a manner? Are there any reading materials you're recommend to explore systems that are less rooted in Abrahamic concepts but also appealing to someone who appreciates the systematic approach of recent Western occultism?

Thanks for hosting these Q&As.

replies.title: Re: Non-Abrahamic magical systems
replies.content: Well, are you comfortable with Druidry? My book The Celtic Golden Dawn presents a full-on system of Golden Dawn-style ceremonial magic which invokes Celtic pagan deities and relies on the philosophy and symbolism of the Druid Revival tradition. The same thing could be done with any other traditional polytheism, for that matter.

replies.title: Re: Non-Abrahamic magical systems
replies.content:
Will I face any roadblocks pursuing magical practice while playing Mad Libs in such a manner?


With all due respect, it's anything but Mad Libs. I can only speak from my own experience with the Druid Magic Handbook, but one has to spend a good chunk of time in discursive meditation before selecting deities for your daily banishing/invocation ritual. Until then, the banishing/invocation by the elements act like training wheels. It is then time for prayer and seeing what deities are willing to help you out, if any.

The Druid pantheon help me out and they are definitely not Abrahamic.

replies.title: Re: Non-Abrahamic magical systems
replies.content:
If JMG will permit me to add something...

I'm going to hazard a guess that your primary exposure to Abrahamic religion has been through conservative Protestantism. That tradition indeed views the Bible as the inerrant, infalliable word of God, presumably handed down by God on a stone tablet to someone somewhere.

In reality, what we call "the Bible" is a collection of writings-some religious, some primarily historical or geneological in nature-written by a series of authors in the Bronze and Iron ages. Some of these people were separated from each other by more than the 2000 years that separate us from Jesus, and their writings illustrate different viewpoints, different cultural contexts, and yes, different beliefs about the nature of God and the Absolute. Nevertheless, they chronicle-albeit in an imperfect way-the spiritual experiences that the Jews and early Christians had with the Divine Absolute, and are thus worth reading and meditating upon. ("Divine Absolute", or something similar, may be better term to use than "God", since, in conservative Protestant contexts, "God" tends to be seen as an angry judgemental old man on a throne in the sky somewhere, which is an...unhelpful way to think of the Divine Foundation of the Universe).

Worship in the early Christian church centered on a Liturgy-a set of hymns and other rituals, which included a reading from the writings of the Jewish prophets, or the writings of the Apostles and early leaders of the church (like Saint Paul). Over time, churches began to establish collections of documents, or in the language of the time, "Books", that were considered acceptable to read during Liturgy-and these collections and their content varied widely from church to church. Indeed, the early church of this time was divided into a vast profusion of groups, with many varying interpretations on the life of Jesus, what he had taught, and what his teachings meant.

This changed when Emperor Constantine came to rule the Roman Empire. Constantine was the first Christian Emperor, but had a decidedly...Imperial vision for his new religion, in which a uniform state church would serve as an ally to, and extension of, Roman imperial power, in much the same way that the cults of Jupiter and the other Roman deities had previously done. Thus, in AD 325, Constantine called a meeting of several hundred Christian bishops who shared his vision, which was held in the city of Nicaea. This meeting, presided over by Constantine himself, came to be known as the "Council of Nicaea", and during its deliberations, the bishops voted on a single authorative version of Christian doctrine, backed by a single authorative set of books that could be read during Liturgy. Christian churches that refused to accept Nicaea's decrees on doctrine and lists of acceptable scripture were thenceforth outlawed, and persecuted with increasing intensity by the Roman Empire and its successor kingdoms until they were wiped out.

Nevertheless, it is important to remember here that even the bishops at Nicaea would not have thought of their collection of approved books (which indeed, came to be known in Greek as Biblia, "the books", hence the word "Bible") as the ultimate authority on Christianity. The Biblia were holy because the bishops-the leaders of the Church, possessing a lineage they traced all the way back to the Apostles-had decreed them so, not the other way around. It would not be until the 1500's-about 1200 years after the decrees of Nicaea-that Luther and Calvin would formulate the doctrine of Sola Scriptura, and thus elevate the Council of Nicaea's creation over the Councilors themselves.

If you read the Bible as the writings of different people, separated over time, who nevertheless experienced the Absolute and gained insights that are of value to you, it will be much more helpful. Furthermore, there are many different ways, even within an Abrahamic context, of experiencing God-to start with, there's Qabala, central to the tradition of ceremonial magic, whose original core was developed by thoroughly orthodox Jews trying to understand the nature of God and the created universe. Its vision of the divine, though starting from the same Old Testament Baptists read, is sharply at variance with the angry, judgemental, condemnatory old man Baptists seem to believe in. Then there's the Islamic Sufis, who sought mystical union with the divine. One Sufi teacher was known to walk through the streets of medieval Baghdad carrying a torch and a pail of water-to burn heaven, and put the fires of hell out (the ultimate message being that people who's desire for God is motivated by a promised reward or fear of punishment don't really want God at all). In Christianity there's the Greek Orthodox Hesychasts, who chant a single prayer, "Lord Jesus Christ, have mercy on me, a sinner", for hours on end, timing it with their heartbeat, until they are literally surrounded by the Uncreated Light of Divine.

Or if you want to be still more rebellious, pick up a copy of the Nag Hammadi writings-the scriptures that the Council of Nicaea didn't want you to read. Basically, there's a whole lot of richness to the Abrahamic tradition that you've probably never heard of, and that in many cases has been deliberately supressed.

comments.title: Conclusions
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

A long time ago, on the comment section in this blog, I set out to learn about whether there was a divination system based on Lewis Spence's Bethluisnon and Bobileth alphabets, described in The Magical Arts in Celtic Britain. You suggested a number of avenues of research.

I've since concluded the following: First, it seems that Lewis Spence never led a Druid Order of his own, though he was a Presider in The Druid Order (ADUB). It doesn't seem like his alphabets were ever used in divination or practical magic. I'm strongly considering adapting it for such use. On that note, I managed to obtain a good copy of the Dosparth Edeyrn Davod Aur, and we as Revival Druids are blessed with many, many alphabets! I've taken a particular liking to Nemnivus' alphabet, which comes with letter meanings, but the winged alphabet is also gorgeous. If we combine these "Welsh alphabets" with the "Irish alphabets", i.e. all the various versions of the Ogham mentioned in the Auraicept na n-Éces and the book of Ballymote, we really have a virtually endless supply to draw from.

Secondarily, I wondered as to whether deities were animalistic or anthropomorphized based on how civilized a civilization became; i.e. if city-dwelling rather than agrarian societies tended towards more anthropomorphized deities.

My conclusion on this is that it seems to be a question of age of a society/religion rather than civilization per se, though these tend to overlap, of course. From my research, it seems like gods start out as abstract forces, not necessarily given any definite form, which are then associated with natural phenomena, either because they are seen as the intelligences of these natural forces, or because these forces are used to represent them. After this point, it seems they are often represented by animals that personify some traits of the deity. This animal tends then to be anthropomorphized in the course of time. Most of the gods worshipped within a civilization seem to take over the duties and symbols of other gods, growing ever more complex in their representations and cults. I think this may be driven by their respective priesthoods, struggling to have ever more all-encompassing gods and drive up temple prominence, donations and attendance (I feel unsure that this is necessarily what the gods themselves desired). A good example of this is the cult of Moritasgus, for instance, though the same effect can also be seen in the Egyptian pantheon over time. Neighboring civilizations obviously also have a strong effect on how gods are represented, famously seen with both the Celts/Romans and Egyptians/Greeks.


Yours in Druidry,

Brigyn
(Scarlet Crunchy Snail)

replies.title: Re: Conclusions
replies.content:
1) Fascinating. I would be delighted to see those alphabets brought into divinatory use! I've found via further research that the system of sound symbolism central to the meanings of the Coelbren seems to have been very widespread -- it derives in large part from Plato's dialogue Cratylus -- and so you could use some such scheme to enrich the meanings of your alphabets. The great challenge I'm facing now is figuring out whether there's a numerical system underlying the Coelbren, to permit Welsh gematria...

2) The movement from theriomorphic gods to anthropomorphic gods is a broad general trend in history, and various ideas have been proposed to account for it. Yours is as plausible as any of the others.

-- Turquiose Palpitating Filbert

replies.title: Re: Astrology question on house rulerships
replies.content:
In a natal chart, which planet rules which house is crucial. comments.title: A Distant Christ
comments.content:
Good morning, JMG! There has been occasional discussion here concerning how the power of the Christian god appears to be waning or, more recently, that he may have shifted his geographical zone of influence. This is something that I have contemplated a great deal, and I have slowly articulated an alternative theory. I think that I am ready to share it now.

In part, this is because I know from my reading that it is not only Africans who are intensely experiencing the presence of Christ. In the Anglican Communion, at least, white Westerners are rapidly becoming the minority as mainstream churches in the “first world” ossify while membership surges in the global South in general. This leads me to believe that something else is going on, and I’m a little surprised that you seem to have missed it, given your body of work; it’s making me second guess myself, so here we are.

Jesus the Rabbi emphatically encouraged his followers to undertake lifestyles defined by simplicity, humility, generosity, and even renunciation. It seems safe to assume that, if Jesus the Rabbi is indeed the avatar of Christ the deity, then said deity is also strongly aligned with these values and their energies.

Meanwhile, contemporary Western culture is founded upon essentially the opposite lived values, whatever claims we may make otherwise. Our lifestyles and enculturation leave precious little space for the operation of anything numinous, let alone this particular deity: not when our society, to be utterly frank, defecates on his precepts and example - again, despite any and all claims to the contrary.

It seems fairly self-evident to me (ironically after much consideration) that it should be difficult to “get in tune” with a power that functions on a wavelength fundamentally incompatible with the energies in which one is immersed. And this isn't a situation like the loveless petitioning Aphrodite with the specific intention of incorporating the god's energies. We have no real desire to change - to repent. Quite the contrary; we put our efforts into redefining Christ to suit ourselves, instead.

And that’s not even taking into account any entirely reasonable exasperation on the part of said god with such unprecedented and widespread hypocrisy - that was another of Jesus's hot-button issues, as the gospels make quite clear. (Of course people regularly flouted Christian principles in earlier eras, as well, but they seem to have been generally less self-deluded about doing so.)

In other words: the problem isn't him, it’s us. Our steadily worsening misalignment with the lived energies of the god we claim to worship seems likely, to me, to be one of the reasons that mainstream Christianity has deteriorated so badly in the modern Westernized nations. But what do you think? Esoterically, is that a plausible scenario?

- Accidental Mystic

replies.title: Re: A Distant Christ
replies.content: It's an interesting speculation. I'd point out, as you've noted, that people regularly flouted Christian principles in earlier eras, and if you read sources from earlier times, they were just as hypocritical about it -- but they were hypocritical in different ways. It might be worth doing a close historical study of the differences, and seeing if that will allow you to figure out what the core issues are. (My guess is that widespread affluence is the crucial issue, but that's just a guess.)

comments.title: Discursive meditation and “big thoughts”
comments.content:
I’ve recently begun a discursive meditation process, and am wondering if certain thought patterns of mine are helpful for hurtful.

I’m someone who naturally tends towards day dreaming, and whose day dreaming naturally tends towards deep thoughts.

At first I used personal topics I wanted to think more deeply about (I practice a self directed therapy called internal family systems, and get a lot of fodder around thinking of the nature of my parts.) After rereading your advice that smaller and more precise themes are more successful, I started meditating on my daily tarot card.

My issue is rather than wandering towards something inane, when my mind wanders it seems like it’s going in a productive direction. The other day, for example, I started meditating on the ace of wands, and found myself thinking about the very nature of creation (comparing good to negative evil as presented in cosdoc for example.) If these are the nature of my wandering thoughts, should I be following them, or should I be trying to reign them in even more (perhaps, as you mentioned in one of your posts, thinking on a particular aspect of the card in question.)

Thank you for moderating such a lovely community!

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation and “big thoughts”
replies.content: So long as you can relate the thoughts back to the theme, it's fine. Let's say you start with the Ace of Wands and you end up thinkiing about the nature of creation. Pick up the habit of asking yourself at intervals, "okay, how does this connect to the Ace of Wands?" Do this systematically and you'll find that your thoughts become a series of creative loops that leave the theme and then circle back to it -- and those make for good meditations.

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation and “big thoughts”

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The SoP works very differently from the LBRP. Notice how much more focused the banishings are; you're not banishing everything, you're banishing specific things.

2) They're astral patterns. You might see if you can find some catchy tunes you like that will chase them away; I have a couple of favorite pieces of Wagner that do that very effectively.

3) No -- if anything, you may become more conscious of things that you're banishing, because they're coming out of the unconscious in order to be banished.

6) Journaling is a good way to get in touch with the emotional pressures that lead to such things. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, the personal unconscious -- another word for the Shadow -- has, broadly speaking, two classes of components. The first are things that you shoved down there in childhood because they were too painful for you to deal with at the time. The second are things that you shove donw there -- notice the difference in verb tenses -- because you don't want to admit that they're true about you. These things are inevitably tangled up together, but they're different in origin and they require different treatment.

Childhood traumas and miseries need to be remembered. You'll find that as you remember them, acknowledge them, and let yourself feel the emotions connected with them, they extract themselves from unconsciousness and become ordinary memories, and the various psychological problems involved with them go away. By contrast, suppressed elements of your character need to be admitted and accepted. You have to accept that, yes, what you've been hating in other people is something you do, too; you have to let yourself experience whatever negative emotions are tied up in that -- and then they become ordinary parts of your character and you can control them consciously.

replies.content: Back in the day, when banks didn't have deposit insurance and so used to go bankrupt (ever thought about that word?) very often, one of the few secure ways to store wealth was to bury it. comments.title: Sensing the life force

Last MM, I asked why the life force, very palpable during the SoP, wasn't felt all the time. You said we have been taught as children to ignore it in western societies. That said, we do talk about what we experience, and there's a wide range.

On to my question of the week: Eldest senses a couple place or nature entities that I have long infered exist by consideration of the area and their influences, but I cannot sense. The entities I directly sense he can't sense at all. (He's late teens.) Being musicians, we talk about it in terms of range. replies.title: Re: Sensing the life force
replies.content: Much of what's done to close off awareness of the life force in children is a matter of omission, not overt suppression. We don't have a common word for the life force in English. (Most other languages do have such a word.) We don't talk about it. If a child expresses some perception based on it, the parents laugh and talk about how cute it is, and if the child perseveres, he's told that it's all in his imagination. Most of the damage is done by the time of our first memories.

replies.title: Re: Sensing the life force

What do the elements get out of it? I thank them for their blessings and then wonder if I should be doing something more substantial to show my gratitude. Currently I am doing it without a pantheon because I haven't settled on one yet (or developed any relationships with gods).

replies.title: Re: Sensing the life force
replies.content:
1) Yes.

2) Elemental spirits, unlike most other beings, depend on us for their spiritual evolution. They are "creations of the created," and lack the inner point of contact with the transcendent that Dion Fortune calls a Divine Spark. As we interact with them, that experience conditions them and allows them to evolve. This is why many Western occult traditions make a point of working with elementals, and establishing good relations with them -- it's beneficial for both sides.

replies.title: Elementals in the CGD
replies.content: Are the animals identified in the CGD's LBRPs elementals, or animals associated with the respective gods of the quarters?

replies.title: Re: Elementals in the CGD
replies.content: Neither one. They're symbols of the elements.

replies.title: Re: Sensing the life force
replies.content:
The fact that we don't actually have convenient words for such things in English plays a huge role in our culture's discomfort -- not to use a stronger word -- with people who perceive them, and who dare to talk about those perceptions. Here's a relevant quote from one of the most profound thinkers (Edward Sapir) about human language in the early 20th century:

"Human beings do not live in the objective world alone, nor alone in the world of social activity as ordinarily understood, but are very much at the mercy of the particular language which has become the medium of expression for their society. It is quite an illusuion to imagine that one adjusts to reality essentially without the use of language and that language is merely an incidental means of solving specific problems of communication or reflection. The fact of the matter is that the ‘real world’ is to a large extent unconsciously built up on the language habits of the group. No two languages are ever sufficiently similar to be considered as representing the same social reality. The worlds in which different societies live are distinct worlds, not merely the same world with different labels attached."

And of course that for which we have no name will remain unknown and frightening. As H. P. Lovecraft sagely remarked, "The oldest and strongest emotion of mankind is fear, and the oldest and strongest kind of fear is fear of the unknown."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) If you're going to work with AODA, yes, you should work with the DMH and/or Dolmen Arch rather than the Celtic Golden Dawn. The DMH/Dolmen Arch are based on the same practices and symbolism AODA uses; the CGD is a completely different system, which is why it has its own organization to support its practice.

The nastiest, most vindictive, most over-the-top abuse I've ever received because of my spiritual beliefs came, not from Christians, not from rationalist atheists, but from Celtic Reconstructionists who insisted that they and they alone had the right to say how people could worship Celtic deities, and responded with shrill nastiness to the very existence of the Druid Revival tradition. I know that some of the other, non-Celtic Reconstructionist movements are saner, and if you consider Heathenry Reconstructionist -- labels vary -- I've always had very good relations with the Heathen community; that said, I'm very wary of anybody who claims the Reconstructionist label.

2) In my experience, the Brythonic Celtic deities are one thing and the Goidelic Celtic deities are something completely different. You can blend Welsh and Gallo-Roman -- lots of Druids do that -- but the Irish gods belong to a different worldview.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Old-fashioned bodybuilding and physical culture had occultism and alternative spirituality all through it, and some very famous names in the early 20th century exercise field were involved to one extent or another. You might look up Joe Greenstein aka The Mighty Atom, who was a serious Cabalistic occultist as well as a professional strongman, or Genevieve Stebbins, one of the main figures in women's exercise, who was one of the leaders of the Brotherhood of Light, a major occult order. To become a competent bodybuilder or weightlifter, you have to train your will and you have to be able to imagine your goals clearly in order to achieve them; you need the same skills to become a capable occultist or operative mage -- and so a significant number of people back in the pre-steroid era did both.

It's a function of karmic blowback. Since money is a zero-sum game, for you to get money without earning it, someone else has to earn money without getting it. I've long since lost track of the number of people I know who did magic to get money for nothing, and ended up being mugged, or robbed, or having their car totaled and the insurance company wouldn't pay a cent, or their bank cashed a fraudulent check for a couple of thou and they couldn't get the bank to admit that it was fraudulent, etc., etc.

replies.title: Re: shamanic counselor

In general, while I find New Age fakery rather dull, it's usually pretty harmless, and can be a fun entertainment and a source of comfortable psychodrama for a lot of people. Be very careful of getting caught up in the same mindset that produced the Amazing Randi...

replies.title: Re: shamanic counselor

comments.title: Possibly Psychic Child
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG. I hope all is well in chilly Rhode Island.

I have an almost 5 year old son. In the past year or so I realised that he would expect people to hear what he was thinking. We'd call it him talking in his head. I figured this is just typical little person not quite knowing how to communicate and stuck in his own head.

What I hadn't quite picked up on was that he seems to be randomly picking up other people's interior thoughts. I've noticed this in the last few weeks since we had to start homeschooling. He would seem to hear stuff I thought about. But it was always a bit vague - I couldn't confirm to myself if he could. Today I got confirmation of this when I thought of something, but said something different. He was unhappy that I was saying two different things! And he told me what my original thought was. I asked him if he could hear other people's talking in their heads and he said yes. As it stands, he seems to pick up what people are randomly thinking, not directed thoughts (I tried).

So my question is this - he's not yet 5, but how do I help him protect himself? It took me years to be able to protect myself properly (I'm very sensitive to other people's emotional states) and I want to give good guidance on how to live with this.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Possibly Psychic Child
replies.content: Hmm. Never having raised a child, I'm by no means an expert on this subject. The people I know who grew up in families with a tradition of psychism all tell me that rule #1 was that you don't talk about such things outside the family, because other people won't understand; inside the family, when no outsiders are around, you can talk freely about it. The most important thing is that the child gets validation for his experiences -- if he knows he can turn to you and talk about it, even if he can't mention it to anyone else, it won't be an emotional burden. Beyond that -- well, here again, I'm not naturally psychic, and the methods of protection appropriate to a trained occultist are not necessarily those that work well with a natural psychic. Anyone else?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I've been thinking about a metaphor for developing on the mental plane. Assume consciousness is a rope we're playing with. So when we develop more and more on the mental plane, we increase the length of rope we have to play with, and increase the chances of tying ourselves into knots. Knots would be general confusion, or unease of consciousness. I'm not talking gross manifestations here, but subtle undercurrents that tug our thoughts and feelings in contradicting directions. Not necessarily causing gross harm, but altering our subtle states of feeling, thinking, and meaning when we stop distracting ourselves and turn inwards. A strong will could quickly pull the rope straight and free it of knots, and that's why developing the will is of vital importance.

a) Does this metaphor make sense to you?
b) I take it that the subtle movement of feeling, thinking, and meaning in contradicting directions is simply a step on the path of magical practice that we might revisit time and time again, in the case, that we're triggering changes as states of consciousness, and not gross manifestations in the physical world
c) Forgive me, I think I'm using this question as an attempt to unknot myself from a state of internal brooding. And trying to verify it's part of the path, in an attempt to convince myself I should be allowed to feel this way.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, except the bit about pulling on the rope. If you do that you'll just set the knots more firmly. Strength of will has to be balanced with clarity and compassion, so that you can pick the knots apart one at a time.

2) Yes, and you can take it further than that. Being pulled in several directions by thoughts and feelings is an utterly normal human experience, because thoughts and feelings reflect different planes of being and affect different aspects of your subtle bodies.

3) Please, as quickly as you can, ditch the mistaken notion that there's any feeling you're not allowed to feel. That's like saying that you're not allowed to see the color orange. Your feelings are what they are; if you've been taught that some of them are wrong or invalid or what have you, the person or people who taught you that was doing a sleazy manipulative number on you. You don't have to act on your feelings, nor do you have to believe them -- and of course the same goes for your thoughts -- but what you feel is what you feel, full stop, end of sentence.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Surely one can chose not to have certain feelings. For example, if one is out driving a car, and the vehicle behind is too close, one can react in the 'normal' way and get angry, or one can chose not to be affected, and carry on feeling calm. Otherwise, one is always going to be at the mercy of other people pushing your buttons, whether deliberately or not. Certainly, one should not allow random other people to dictate what feelings are right or wrong (one should get angry if someone is being 'racist', etc.), but the idea that one has no control at all over one's own feelings - I hope that's not what you're suggesting - is really anathema, as far as I'm concerned. One recall's Dion Fortune's well-known description of the character of the Initiate, for example. The same goes for thoughts, of course - I decide what I think, and banish any thoughts which don't come from me, unless I'm deliberately inviting them. That should really go without saying, IMO.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG, what did witches do that led to the myth of their flying on broomsticks?

—Lady Cutekitten

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
According to the only writers I know of who've made a convincing case, it's a custom from very old traditions of European shamanism, which involved the use of potent psychoactive herbs. Those herbs were boiled in fat to make a flying ointment, and the practitioner would take off her clothing, rub the ointment on the broomstick, place it where the ointment would be absorbed by certain mucous membranes, and dance a wild ceremonial dance while straddling the broom until she had absorbed enough of the drug to start, ahem, flying. The hallucinatory events described in the legends of witchcraft duly followed, at least in the minds of those affected.

I don't recommend trying this at home.

replies.title: flying ointment
replies.content:
I know someone who came very close to killing herself by experimenting with certain herbs. She had the "brilliant" idea of mixing the herbal extracts with pharmaceutical penetrating cream instead of lard or goose grease as tradition called for. She got the barest trace of the mixture under her fingernail and started feeling the effects, realized how dangerous it was and called 911.

There are some herbalists who make less potent mixtures sold as flying ointment. I have used some, don't recall the brand, but didn't get much from them except a mild buzz. Your mileage may differ. I recall reading something Robert Anton Wilson wrote about a pretty wild trip from belladonna, which was one of the traditional witches herbs, along with hensbane and wolfsbane.

Rita

A sort of hyperbolic realization of a lot of my own neuroses. I’ve since transitioned to traditional Golden Dawn, and am initiating through the neophyte grade. Things have since dramatically evened out, and especially in the last few weeks I’ve been finding remarkable improvement in different areas in my life.

Do you know what that initial turn could have been about? You’ve mentioned parasitic forces or spirits that bind themselves to individuals, could something like that have doubled down when it recognized my attempts at banishing and purifying?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, that may have been at least part of what was going on. There's also a process known as karmic culmination -- when you start serious magical or spiritual training, any unfinished karma you have hanging around tends to become active all at once. If you keep going, it settles back down, but a lot of people have a few harrowing weeks first!
